title: Three Months
html:
The letter is two pages.

I read it standing in the upstairs corridor with Lily holding the lamp, because I did not want to take it to my room first and I did not want to read it on the stairs. The corridor has a window at the far end that lets in the last of the evening light. It is not quite enough to read by. I read it anyway and let Lily's lamp fill the gap.

The first page is formal ceremony language. Every noble of the empire who turns 16 in year 999 is cordially invited to the annual coming-of-age ceremony, hosted by the imperial household on the 14th of the second month, to be held in the Sunlit Hall of the Galahelm Palace. Dress formal. Arrival by the second bell. The list of required documentation includes a copy of the house seal and a confirmation of attendance, to be submitted to the palace steward's office no later than ten days prior.

Standard. Fine. Already on the plan.

I turn to the second page.

It is shorter, written in a different hand from the first. The same seal at the top, but the tone shifts from ceremonial to administrative. The imperial household wishes to inform the house of Rosette that, pursuant to the Imperial Title Review Ordinance, the scheduled review of the Rosette County title, previously set for the fifth month of year 999, has been moved forward to the third month of year 999. The review will be conducted by the Imperial Lands Bureau in conjunction with the Ministry of Nobility. All relevant documentation regarding the house's current status, holdings, and service record should be submitted to the Ministry of Nobility by the 28th of the second month.

The 28th of the second month is fourteen days after the ceremony.

I fold the letter along its original crease, hold it for a moment, and give it back to Lily.

"Put it with my correspondence," I say. "I'll deal with it in the morning."

She takes it without comment. I go downstairs.

***

Father is in the study at the back of the ground floor, which is where he goes in the evenings when he is working through something he does not want to bring to the dinner table. The door is half open and the lamp inside is the small one, the kind he uses when he does not expect company. I knock on the frame and push the door the rest of the way.

He looks up. The expression on his face is the one that has been sitting underneath his normal expression all day, and now that there is no table between us and no meal to redirect toward, it does not have anywhere to go.

"Elena."

"May I sit down?"

He gestures at the chair across the desk. I sit.

The desk has papers on it, which is normal for an evening in the study. What is not normal is the way they are stacked, which is the way papers get stacked when someone is not working on them but wants to look like they might be. I glance at the stack without pointing at it.

"I received a letter this evening," I say. "From the imperial household. Two pages. The first was the ceremony invitation. The second was a notice that the title review has been moved forward. Third month, not fifth."

He is very still.

"I saw you set a letter face-down at breakfast this morning," I continue. "Same seal. I would like to know what it says."

A silence. He looks at the stack of papers on the desk, then at me, then at the wall beside the window, in the specific sequence of someone who has been rehearsing this conversation since this morning and has arrived at the point in the rehearsal where the other person has already said most of the difficult lines.

"I received it yesterday evening," he says. "After dinner." He reaches into the stack without looking and produces the letter, which he sets flat on the desk between us. He does not push it toward me. "I wanted to tell you this morning. I didn't know how."

I pick it up and read it.

It is longer than mine, addressed to the Count of Rosette rather than to Elena personally, and it covers more ground. The title review date is confirmed, moved forward by two months per a routine calendar adjustment by the Ministry of Nobility. Fine. I have that. What I do not have is the paragraph below it, which thanks the Count of Rosette for his family's long service to the empire and notes, with what the writer clearly intends as gentleness, that the Ministry anticipates a straightforward review given the family's current status. The word straightforward is doing a great deal of work in that sentence. It means the Ministry does not expect the Rosette family to give them anything useful to work with, and they are being polite about saying so in writing.

The last paragraph is the one that takes me a moment.

It is a personal note, apparently from the author of the letter rather than the Ministry officially, which the shift in register signals before the words do. It states that the author has heard, through temple correspondence, of a development in the house of Rosette that he found remarkable, and that he hopes the family is well. He does not specify what development. He signs with a name I do not recognize, a title I do not recognize, and a postscript that says only: I trust the house will make good use of what it has been given.

I set the letter down.

'Temple correspondence.' The priest. Not a slow leak then. A fast one. The letter was sent the same day I tested, or the day after at the latest, fast enough that Father received it before I told him about the mana result myself.

"Someone at court already knows," I say.

"Apparently." Father's voice is even. He has had all day to arrive at even. "I don't know who sent it. The name on the letter is a minor functionary's. Someone used a minor functionary."

Which means whoever sent it preferred not to put their own name on a letter to a family this far down the social hierarchy, yet made sure the letter arrived. Someone paying attention to the Rosette family who did not want to be visible about it. I file that under unverified and move on.

"The review is in three months," I say. "Not five."

"Yes."

"That changes the schedule."

"Elena." He says my name in the voice he uses when he is about to say something he has been holding carefully. "You don't have to..." He stops. "You have the water affinity. That is enough. That, by itself, changes what the Ministry will find when they review us. I can begin the formal application to the Blue Mage Tower. They may take time to review it, but having the application on record before the Ministry meets. It demonstrates intent. It shows the family is not standing still."

He means well. He also means: you do not have to do any of the rest of it. Not the swordsmanship, not the ceremony, not whatever it is you are building in your room with those five books. You have enough.

I think about that for a moment.

Three months is enough time to reach tier 1 water magic, probably, if the first session's result is a reliable indicator of what this body can do. Tier 1 means I can produce water on demand. That is not an impressive demonstration on its own. A Blue Mage Tower application from a tier 1 mage with no training history, no lineage connection to the tower, and no letter of sponsorship is a file that sits in a queue for a year before anyone looks at it, assuming it is not returned for insufficient documentation first. The application demonstrates intent. It does not demonstrate viability.

What demonstrates viability is the ceremony. Specifically, what I do at the ceremony.

"The application is a good idea," I say. "Start it. But I'd like to go to the ceremony first and give the Ministry something to write about in their report."

He looks at me for a long moment.

"What are you planning to do at the ceremony?"

"Not embarrass the family."

"Elena."

"Be visible." I keep my voice practical. "The court has an opinion about this family. I'd like to introduce them to a different data point before the Ministry sits down to write their assessment. Nothing dramatic. Just present and noticeable." I pause. "The mana result helps. But only if the people who need to factor it in have actually seen me."

He sits back. The careful expression on his face is doing something more complicated now, which I think is what hope looks like when it is trying to stay proportionate.

"All right," he says. "All right."

"Get the sword instructor as quickly as you can," I add. "Three months is workable. Five was more comfortable."

"I'll send the letters tonight."

I stand and push the chair back. "Thank you for telling me."

He looks up at me. "I should have told you this morning."

"You told me now." I pick up his letter from the desk and hold it out. "File this somewhere useful. I may need to reference the name."

He takes it. I leave the study and go upstairs.

***

The magic lamp is already on when I reach my room, which means Lily came up ahead of me and switched it on, which she does on the evenings when she has gauged that I will want to sit and think rather than go directly to bed. I have no idea how she reads this, but she has not been wrong yet.

I sit at the desk and take out the diary.

Three months. Revision required.

The ceremony is in 29 days. The title review is 14 days after that. That is 43 days from now to the review submission deadline, with the ceremony itself sitting in the middle as the one significant opportunity I have to change the court's default assumption about this family before the Ministry finalizes its paperwork. The original timeline had 5 months to build something. Three months means the same destination and a shorter road.

I write the revised dates and set the pen down.

The first mana practice session produced a teacup. The second session, tonight, produced nothing worth recording: I spent 40 minutes with the faint pressure behind my eyes, tried three times to pull a thread outward, and got a small damp patch on my palm that dried before I could measure it. Progress that goes backward before it goes forward is not news. I write it down anyway.

The sword instructor situation is pending.

The dressmaker appointment is tomorrow.

The etiquette sessions continue.

The priest is a variable I cannot currently control. Whoever used him to send that letter to court is a variable I cannot currently identify. I add both to the unverified column and close the diary.

Outside, the garden is dark and the outer wall is a pale line at the edge of the lamp's reach. I sit there for a moment with my hands flat on the desk, looking at it.

Three months is enough. I have worked with less.

I close the lamp and go to bed.
